prompt:
Create a single avant-garde photomontage poster using REFERENCE_0 as the main subject and raw photo material, with REFERENCE_1 only as additional nightlife/flash-photography texture inspiration if needed. Transform the reference into a fusion of 1920s constructivist design and 1970s punk-zine collage: the tension between a clean geometric grid and rough cut-and-paste paper should be the main visual idea.

Canvas and composition: Make a vertical poster with aged off-white paper, rough torn blackened edges, visible grain, scuffs, folds, and xerox texture. Reuse the woman from REFERENCE_0 as cropped photo fragments rather than a normal portrait. Arrange exactly 3 photographic cutouts: 1 small tilted street-photo cutout on the left, 1 large tilted close-up cutout in the upper right, and 1 tall narrow shard-like cutout through the lower center. Keep the original neon-night mood inside the photos but recolor it into a limited red, black, cream, and muted flesh-toned palette.

Graphic additions: Add bold constructivist geometry around the collage: exactly 1 large red circle behind the upper-right portrait, exactly 1 small red dot near the bottom right, black diagonal bands/blocks, sharp cream triangular separations, and angular white borders between the photo shards. Add pieces of beige masking tape and torn newsprint scraps to make the collage feel physically pasted together.

Typography: Add large slanted block text reading POP in the upper left, with mixed black and red letters. Add a ransom-note style date reading 1924 in the lower right, each character on separate paper tiles with one red tile. Include one tiny torn-newspaper caption scrap on the left with the text The streets are full of noise and nonsense.

Style constraints: High-contrast poster design, screenprint ink, photocopied punk flyer texture, imperfect alignment, cut-paper shadows, distressed paper fibers, no glossy modern polish, no extra people, no additional readable text beyond the specified 3 text elements.
